ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a clear, novel perspective of predicting video by modeling local affine transformations on tiled patches (Intro; Sec.2.1–2.3) rather than directly regressing pixels, and trains a CNN to predict future transforms with multi-step recurrence. It also proposes a practical evaluation protocol that measures plausibility via a classifier trained on ground-truth sequences (Sec.2.4), which is a distinct contribution to evaluation practice. These ideas are a non-trivial and well-differentiated engineering approach relative to prior pixel-/flow-/adversarial-based methods (Related Work), but they are primarily a new, effective formulation and baseline rather than a deep theoretical breakthrough, so the novelty is notable but not transformative. | B(4): The paper introduces a clear new perspective by predicting local affine transformations on tiled patches instead of raw pixels (Sec.1–2, affine transform extractor/predictor) and by proposing a classifier-based evaluation protocol that measures plausibility rather than pixel MSE (Sec.2.4, experiments). It explicitly contrasts this approach with prior pixel-space, adversarial, and flow-based methods (Related Work) and employs a recurrent/unrolled CNN to make multi-step prediction—a non-trivial but conceptually straightforward technique. Overall the idea is a notable and well-differentiated novelty rather than a purely incremental reuse of existing components.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clear, novel perspective of predicting video by modeling local affine transformations on tiled patches (Intro; Sec.2.1–2.3) rather than directly regressing pixels, and trains a CNN to predict future transforms with multi-step recurrence. It also proposes a practical evaluation protocol that measures plausibility via a classifier trained on ground-truth sequences (Sec.2.4), which is a distinct contribution to evaluation practice. These ideas are a non-trivial and well-differentiated engineering approach relative to prior pixel-/flow-/adversarial-based methods (Related Work), but they are primarily a new, effective formulation and baseline rather than a deep theoretical breakthrough, so the novelty is notable but not transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a clear new perspective by predicting local affine transformations on tiled patches instead of raw pixels (Sec.1–2, affine transform extractor/predictor) and by proposing a classifier-based evaluation protocol that measures plausibility rather than pixel MSE (Sec.2.4, experiments). It explicitly contrasts this approach with prior pixel-space, adversarial, and flow-based methods (Related Work) and employs a recurrent/unrolled CNN to make multi-step prediction—a non-trivial but conceptually straightforward technique. Overall the idea is a notable and well-differentiated novelty rather than a purely incremental reuse of existing components.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper is generally well organized with a clear progression (introduction → related work → model → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions (predicting transforms instead of pixels; a classifier-based evaluation protocol) are stated early. Notation is mostly introduced where needed (affine matrices, patch sizes, strides), but a few expressions are malformed or ambiguous (e.g. "drsr dc sc" instead of a clear product/division) and some variable usage could be tightened. Figures and tables are repeatedly referenced which helps navigation, but several typographical errors, duplicated paragraphs near the end, and occasional grammatical/sloppy sentences reduce readability and merit cleanup. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized (Introduction → Model → Experiments → Conclusion) and states its main contributions early (intro paragraphs clearly describe the transformation-based model and new evaluation protocol). Notation and method descriptions are mostly consistent and understandable, though a few formulations are confusing (e.g. "every pixel participates in drsr dc sc overlapping patches" lacking clear multiplication/spacing) and some variable uses (At/At) are slightly noisy. Figures/tables are referenced appropriately and examples are described, but the manuscript contains distracting editorial issues (repeated paragraphs, stray LaTeX/footnote artifacts like "LaTeX with hyperref package" and duplicated blocks near the end) that undermine polish and could confuse readers. With minor copyediting to remove repetitions and fix small notation/formatting glitches the presentation would be excellent.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized with a clear progression (introduction → related work → model → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions (predicting transforms instead of pixels; a classifier-based evaluation protocol) are stated early. Notation is mostly introduced where needed (affine matrices, patch sizes, strides), but a few expressions are malformed or ambiguous (e.g. "drsr dc sc" instead of a clear product/division) and some variable usage could be tightened. Figures and tables are repeatedly referenced which helps navigation, but several typographical errors, duplicated paragraphs near the end, and occasional grammatical/sloppy sentences reduce readability and merit cleanup.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (Introduction → Model → Experiments → Conclusion) and states its main contributions early (intro paragraphs clearly describe the transformation-based model and new evaluation protocol). Notation and method descriptions are mostly consistent and understandable, though a few formulations are confusing (e.g. "every pixel participates in drsr dc sc overlapping patches" lacking clear multiplication/spacing) and some variable uses (At/At) are slightly noisy. Figures/tables are referenced appropriately and examples are described, but the manuscript contains distracting editorial issues (repeated paragraphs, stray LaTeX/footnote artifacts like "LaTeX with hyperref package" and duplicated blocks near the end) that undermine polish and could confuse readers. With minor copyediting to remove repetitions and fix small notation/formatting glitches the presentation would be excellent.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence (qualitative on moving-MNIST and quantitative evaluation on UCF-101 using a pretrained C3D classifier), and the paper provides a working algorithmic pipeline (affine extraction, CNN predictor, multi-step unrolled training). There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check. Experimental methodology is reasonable (baselines include copy-last-frame, optical-flow extrapolation, and Mathieu et al.), but the paper omits important quantitative details (actual table numbers, variance/statistical tests, full hyperparameter and training details) and relies on a single classifier-based metric whose properties are not fully analyzed. The authors acknowledge key limitations (underestimation of motion from MSE in transform space and simplifying affine/patched assumptions) and the conclusions are generally supported by the presented results, so overall technical soundness is good with minor gaps in experimental reporting. | B(3): The main claims (predicting local affine transforms yields sharper, plausible video predictions and the proposed classifier-based evaluation) are clearly stated and supported by experiments on Moving MNIST and UCF‑101 including comparisons to baselines (copy, optical flow extrapolation, Mathieu et al.). The modeling and training descriptions are internally consistent (patch tiling, joint affine extraction by MSE reconstruction, CNN predictor with multi-step unrolling) and contain no obvious mathematical errors. However, important experimental details are missing or under-specified in the provided text (exact quantitative results/tables are not shown here, no statistical tests, limited description of the affine-extraction optimizer and hyperparameters), and the classifier-based metric can be biased by classifier robustness to distortions — a possible confound not controlled for. Overall the approach appears plausible and partially validated, but some methodological gaps and insufficient reporting weaken the strength of the technical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: Main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence (qualitative on moving-MNIST and quantitative evaluation on UCF-101 using a pretrained C3D classifier), and the paper provides a working algorithmic pipeline (affine extraction, CNN predictor, multi-step unrolled training). There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check. Experimental methodology is reasonable (baselines include copy-last-frame, optical-flow extrapolation, and Mathieu et al.), but the paper omits important quantitative details (actual table numbers, variance/statistical tests, full hyperparameter and training details) and relies on a single classifier-based metric whose properties are not fully analyzed. The authors acknowledge key limitations (underestimation of motion from MSE in transform space and simplifying affine/patched assumptions) and the conclusions are generally supported by the presented results, so overall technical soundness is good with minor gaps in experimental reporting.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (predicting local affine transforms yields sharper, plausible video predictions and the proposed classifier-based evaluation) are clearly stated and supported by experiments on Moving MNIST and UCF‑101 including comparisons to baselines (copy, optical flow extrapolation, Mathieu et al.). The modeling and training descriptions are internally consistent (patch tiling, joint affine extraction by MSE reconstruction, CNN predictor with multi-step unrolling) and contain no obvious mathematical errors. However, important experimental details are missing or under-specified in the provided text (exact quantitative results/tables are not shown here, no statistical tests, limited description of the affine-extraction optimizer and hyperparameters), and the classifier-based metric can be biased by classifier robustness to distortions — a possible confound not controlled for. Overall the approach appears plausible and partially validated, but some methodological gaps and insufficient reporting weaken the strength of the technical claims.
